speaker: interviewer
text: I'm just-- OK, Max, you were just telling us the story of your cigar caper.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there more to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was all kinds of things. Mainly, we, we tried to survive. We did all kind of things that I wouldn't dare do now. But in that time, we did, and we thought it was normal. That's the way we were taught to-- that's the way we teach ourselves to do it, and that was it. I can tell you stories. All the kids had, like, [NON-ENGLISH] how do you call this? How do you say this in English? [NON-ENGLISH] They had the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The chutzpah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sores. We were full of sores, because we didn't have enough soap to wash ourselves. And there were things between the fingers, like this. I don't know the name of this thing. And you know why this happened? Because we used to take a bath once a week. A bath. A regular bath. So we used to go-- they didn't have showers. They had three bathtubs there. So we used to go two, and then the two got out. Two other ones got in, and then two other ones got in.

speaker: interviewer
text: The big-- the strong ones used to take the baths first. I was not in this category. I wasn't that strong. So I was the second shift, and after me was a smaller one yet. This was the third shift. And whatever one had, the rest of them got through that way. This was bad condition for that, but we survived. That's it. So many stories to tell that it's almost unbelievable. But that's-- it's all part, part of the past, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But the main thing, it was no sanitation, very little, and no food. We were starving all day long-- all day long. I think they had fruit, fruit trees there. They never had an apple because we eat them before they grew out. We used to steal the apples and eat them. The carrots, they never could hide them far enough for us because we used to dig out all the carrots in the fields and eat the carrots. Beets-- we used to go in the fields from the peasants and pull out all the beets. They were always complaining. A just reason, because we were eating them. And this was meant for the cows, not for the people. But we were as hungry as the cows, so we were eating it too. But the children, they wanted to eat. That's it. We used to eat two slices of bread a day, I remember, for breakfast. And bread-- black bread, you could take it and throw it against the wall, and it would stick against the wall. So bad, it was because they didn't have anything else. And mainly we eat cabbage because they had a lot of cabbage and herring because there was a lot of herring during the war in Belgium. Everybody was cooking herring. All kind-- I can tell you 10 different ways how to make herring. My mama, I remember, made gefilte fish with herring. Don't ask how it tastes, you know, but at that time, that's what we were eating. And fried herring, and cooked herring, and baked herring. And when I came back from the, the camp, I couldn't look at a cabbage for, I think, five years in a row because that's all we had, every day, every day, every day cabbage, cabbage, cabbage, and herring. This was really something.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said there were like a hierarchy of strong-- the strong group, and the lesser, and the lesser.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the way it went.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was there infighting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely. Definitely. I was very lucky because I ran extremely fast. They couldn't catch me. So I didn't get beat too often. But when they did get me, then they let me have it because the stronger ones took from the other ones. And they wanted to-- they wanted to rule over the younger ones, you know? That's the way. It's part of life. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then the things is-- the one thing is good about it that we stayed there for two, three years. Then we grew up to their size, you know, so we could give them back after, you know? But there was really a hierarchy, like you said. But it's so far away that I forgot about it. How many of you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was at least 100 children there in this Cul-des-Sarts. At least 100 boys and girls. If not 100, not far from it. Quite a few kids. Quite a few. And then people to take care of us, and regular teacher. We used to go to school every day. And then one day, they came and got me. In the middle of from nowhere, they came and picked me. I don't know why. They took me, went back to Brussels with a woman that came and got me. Stayed a couple days in Brussels, and then they took me back a town next to the village where I was, Couvin. This was a school where you become a teacher. And they put me in there, maybe because I was good in school or something. I don't know. But they put me with the older children. I mean, not children. I was in that time already 14. And the other ones were pre-- pre-teachers, like a junior high. I mean like a junior college, it was. That was with people 17, 18 years old. And i was following the courses with them, but I didn't count. I didn't get no papers or anything like that. And I stayed there. Matter of fact, that's where-- that's where the Americans came in. And then I went back to Cul-des-Sarts with my father. And my father picked me up from over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what h-- so you were in Couvin?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Couvin when the Americans came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that. How did-- how did it happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, it was about-- from Couvin to Cul-des-Sarts, a tramway used to go between the two. So it wasn't very far. I used to come back for the weekends. And I remember I came back to Cul-des-Sarts when the Americans came into there because it was a-- the-- [INAUDIBLE]. The first thing I met-- an American came in. And it was a Jewish fellow. His name was Berkowitz, and he was from New York. And he was the mess officer because the soldiers were not far away from us, but some were wandering around.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he came, and he saw there was Jewish children there. He said, I'm coming back. He went there to his-- where he was, the camp, the soldiers. And he came back with an ambulance full of things for the children. Toothbrushes and soap and chocolate and chewing gum. He was so thrilled to see Jewish children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then there was a few guys was from Poland. One was speaking Polish to me, and he said, I'm from Poland. You don't have to be afraid any more. I'm American, but from Poland. Speak Polish to me because you're Polish descendants. I said, I know, my parents, but not me. I couldn't understand him. He was so disappointed, this guy, I remember. And then my father came, and then I went back to Brussels, back to a normal activity. But the kids stayed there because they had to wait until people came and claimed them because most of them didn't have no parents no more. So then the Jewish organization got involved. They got organized, and took out all those children. And they made camps day camps in Brussels-- schools-- for those kids. And whoever wanted to go to school, they teach them trades or school. The Jewish organizations, they did this for the Jewish children after the war. They got already help from all over. And then whoever had family in Israel, or in America, because then the kids started to disperse themselves. But they had home for children for at least two years after the war in Brussels.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that moment like for you when your father came to get you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was out of the blue. I didn't expect that. Then all of a sudden, I see my father. Because my father, he, he went-- he did-- nothing stopped him. There was no train, no nothing, but he made it. He came. He was hitchhiking with the soldiers, you know, and he came all the way there to pick us up. So when we came in this village, the first thing we did is say, let's go back, but let's go back with something because in Brussels, they didn't have nothing. So in there, the peasants thought the war was over. So they had all this butter and the flour, and they didn't know what to do it. They were hiding it for the black market.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father bought it for two times nothing. Whatever I had in a rucksack, we threw everything away, and we filled it with butter, I remember. Because over there, let's say you buy it for $1. We sold it for $100. So much of a difference. And then we went back to Brussels, and we bought all this stuff and we sold it because my dad didn't have nothing there no more. This was his first dollars that he made after the war. And then we went back again, and we made a few trips back and forth. Used to come back we meat, with butter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then every time I used to go back, I used to take back a child. Another one of my friends that we made out a letter that the parents want them. It wasn't even true. But we took-- they had a sister, or things like that. Whoever had relatives, I took back at least three, four kids, back and forth like this because they didn't want to give up the children because they didn't know what's going to become of them until, until the things got more to the normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the first thing your father said to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was happy to see me alive. I don't remember what he said. I'm sure he hugged me because my dad loved me. He was a very, very good dad, my father. He was exceptionally a good man. Didn't have much money, but he had a lot of love to give. That's for sure. I can only tell good things about him-- a very good father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what had happened to your brother and sister at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my brother was a little tiny boy when the war started. He  was born, I think, in '38. So he was about a year and a half old when all this thing started. And they placed him in a home for blind children. This I remember. I know because they told me after. And he was the leader of the pack. He was the only seeing child with all the children blind. And he was leading the children. And he was at least two years there. And the-- it's nuns were taking care of this particular school.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Brussels?

speaker: interviewee
text: Under Brussels somewhere. I don't remember exactly where it was. It was about 20 miles from Brussels. I know one thing. When we came-- when my father picked me up and we came back to Brussels, we went to see-- to get my brother. And they wouldn't release him. They wouldn't give him to us because they didn't know who we were. And we went-- we went with our bicycles over there, so we decided to kidnap my brother. And while they were walking with all the kids, we grabbed him, we put him on the bike, and we took off. And this kid was screaming. He didn't know who we were because he did not recognize us. And we kidnapped him. We really kidnapped him. We took him back home. And then after-- they came after and then we straightened out the whole story. But they wouldn't release him because they had-- maybe they were planned to convert him if the parents don't come back, and stuff like that. This I know from my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: My sister, she was in a convent during the war. And my father went to get her, too. I didn't have much in common with my sister because I didn't even know at all where she was. And she was older than me. So she had her life to tell, you know? So too much, I don't know about it. But I know that she was in a convent for a long, long time. And then she came back, too. My father went to get her after, and he brought her back. He took everybody out. This I can tell you about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was not easy after the war, because there was no jobs available and everything. After a while-- after a while, we start going again. But the main thing, we were free. This was important. We were afraid for a while when they had the offensive on Ostend. We were afraid the German were coming back at the end of '44 when there was the offensive. And they-- you know, they were in Bastogne where I was hiding in the seminar. That's where the Germans were-- where [INAUDIBLE] Ostend were when they came and attacked in the Ardennes in Belgium. But after a while, the Americans took over. And then they went all the way to, to finish them up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your family know, at the time, what was going on-- what was happening to Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have a little story to tell you about that. In 1943, there was one guy. His name is Willie Erkowitz. He was-- he was older than me because he was a married man when they deported him. But he ran away from Auschwitz. He's the only Jew that I know that ran away from camp. And he came-- and the reason why he ran away, he had a friend of his that had a German girlfriend. And she helped to set up the escape and bought him clothing and stuff like that. And he came all the way to Belgium from during the war on his way to Switzerland. And whoever he could see and find-- Jewish people-- he told them. They burn the Jews. Don't go in the street. Hide in a hole if you have to. But don't, don't be caught because if you're caught, 99% you're not going to come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's what happened. Because out of 10 Jews in Belgium, one came back. The rest of them never made it. The children never made it for sure because they went straight to the ovens. They didn't have no-- you don't know about the Jewish children that survived-- very, very seldom that you heard of one, you know? So he-- the Jews knew, but they didn't believe it. They just wouldn't believe it, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can tell you something. Right after the war when people start to come back from the camps-- so we had like a Jewish org-- a Jewish organization there for the [INAUDIBLE]. So my mom used to go every day there to talk to people that came out from camp to see she finds a relative or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she found-- she came home with a, a kid-- he must have been about 19, something like that-- that survived the war. And she took him home to eat and stuff. And then, while he was eating, my mom said, do you know about this one? Because he was from the same town as my mother. So he said, in the oven. My mom didn't know what it meant, you know? And whoever she asked, he said, in the oven. And so my mom said, what, what you talking, in the oven? Where is that? So he got up. He got so mad, the guy. Are you stupid or something? Don't you know what an oven is? They burn you over there. My mom couldn't believe it, you see. And that's the way we really find out because during the war, the Jews didn't believe it. But after the war, unfortunately, we found out, and fast, you know? I remember. I see the guy in front of my eyes. He's in Israel somewhere now. Where had he been?

speaker: interviewee
text: In camp, during the war, you know. I don't know which camp, you know, but he was from Poland, from the same town that my mother, and they came in. That's it. After this, this was my years that I cannot remember. There's so much things that I don't remember everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after the war, then, now you're, what, 14?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, I'm 15 already.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're 15. So what happened next in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to begin with, my father wanted me to go to school. And I said, there's no way that I can go to school when there is not a dime in the house. How can I ask you to feed me and everything without me working? So I didn't want to, and I went to work. My dad wanted to, but I didn't. So I went to work, and whatever I was-- meager salary I was making, I was helping whatever I could. I was an apprentice electrician, I remember. I was working.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I joined HaShomer Hatzair, and, and there, again, I had to do a lot with the youth movement in Brussels. And I was in HaShomer Hatzair for about three years, something like that. Yeah, for about three. And then I learned a lot about Jews because on the way that the HaShomer Hatzair teaches you because a little bit on the left side, you know? But it was very good to be a Jew. You became proud to be a Jew, by you go out from HaShomer Hatzair.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I stayed there for about three years, and, and then I quit this too because they became too possessive for me, of mine opinion. You couldn't do anything you wanted anymore. You belonged to the HaShomer Hatzair. They were really indoctrine-- how you say that-- indoctrine you. And they wanted too much from me. And they wouldn't give me my freedom. And I had enough from during the war, and everything, so I said no. That's not for me. And I walked out, period. And then I went. I was older already, and then I went to the army in Belgium, stayed there a couple years, and came out and got married. And then during the meantime, my mother passed away. She died a very young woman. And I remained with my dad after the war. And then I met my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my wife is also-- I met her in HaShomer Hatzair when she was a young girl. She's younger than me, so at the time I didn't pay attention to her. She was too young for me. But after I met her, we got together, and then we got married. And that's second part of my life, you know? So what year did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married in 1952. I was not-- I was about-- how old was I? I was-- not-- I wasn't 22 years old, something like that. I got married in January, and I got 22 in April. And my wife was 18 when we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name? Your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's name is Hena-- Helen. That's easier, Helen. And we lived in Brussels, and we had a little store. And in that time, my sister moved to America already. And my brother lived in America too because my father was very sick. So we had a aunt in New York, and she says, why don't you send the kid over here and I take care of it. So my father sent my brother because we thought that we're all going to leave and go to America later. But then it didn't work out for my father. He got remarried in the meantime, and she had children there, so she couldn't go. And he didn't go. And then my sister came, and then the-- this aunt in New York didn't want my brother anymore. So she moved to California, and took her brother-- the brother with her and lived in Los Angeles. And then she made the papers for us, and then we came to Los Angeles in 1956. And at that time, we had already a little girl too. We had a child that was born in '54. And we came to America, and then that's another story.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came to America in '54?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '56. My daughter was born in '54, and then we came in January of 1956. We came to Los Angeles. And we're here ever since.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you do in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to begin with, I worked for Desmond's when I came to Los Angeles. I was an alteration man. I know-- I know about tailoring, so I was working in the shop there. And then I was a fitter for them. And then I got on my own, and I worked for an upholster part time. I was sewing cushions at night. And then the guy sold the business, so I bought it without any money. I didn't have any money yet. But I found a guy that wanted to go partners with me that had the money. He put up the money, and I put up the knowledge. And we were together for about seven years, and then I bought him out. He went on his own, and I went on my own. And I'm ever since, I'm in the upholstering. And I took-- I bought the shop. And all the children got born, and we stayed here. At least at that time, we were-- we were a free man and woman. We didn't have this problem we had over there. And we're in America ever since.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many more children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have-- my oldest daughter, her name is Claudine. She was born in '54. Then I have a son. His name is Brian. He's born in '58. And then I have another daughter. Her name is Natalie. And she's born-- wait a second, I have to remember-- in '63. She's born in '63. And her name is Natalie because she carries the name of my father. My father's name was Nathan, and her name is Natalie. And she's born the same day as me. The 18th of April, so I know for sure. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How about-- how about Jewish life for you? Are you involved in any?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I am involved in a certain way because I belong to a group of people from Europe, and we call ourselves Continental 22. And those are all people from Poland, and from Belgium, and from France. It's a group of people. In our own way, you know, we, we put money together for Israel. We, we send money to an orphanage over there. And indirectly, I help the best I can, and that's about all.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first realize that something different did happen to you in your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, that, that something actually happened in your life that, that affected you.

speaker: interviewee
text: During the war, I thought it was part of my life like this because the Germans came in in 1940, and they were not bad with the Jews. They let everybody keep on with their own life. It's in '41, '42 that start to, to become sour. But then they sent us away to those, those home for children, and that was it. See, this was part of my life. I thought that's the way it's supposed to be. So there was no transition. It was just a continuation for me. So I didn't realize it was bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: I only realized that I was a Jew when I start-- when I belonged to the HaShomer Hatzair and that I learn about my ancestry and everything that I realized what the Jew means to be, you know? But during the war, we knew that the Germans don't like the Jews. But I wasn't too much-- I wasn't a religious Jew or anything like that. I used to go, like I say, and hide it to learn Jewish. But this was part of my normal life, see. So, to me, there was no jump from one to another. Just kept on going. And did you tell your children about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I, I told everything because I'm very-- that's why I'm very happy that we're making this particular film because they always say, why don't you tell about your life, you know? So this way they will have a little bit of a chance to know what was going on, see? And they're very anxious to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they have their own lives, too. They're all married now. And they have their own children. My oldest daughter has two children. His name-- one is, is Jonathan. And the girl, her name is Erin. My son has a son. His name is Alex. And they're already big kids too-- nine years old and 12 years old. What else you-- what else should I say? What, what would you want them to know about-- what would you want your grandchildren to know about the experiences that you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- the-- they keep on asking, you know? Not the smallest one, but my grandson is very much interested because he goes to, to Hebrew school. And he learn what it is-- same thing I learned in the HaShomer Hatzair, he learns in Hebrew school. And he know what went on during the war, so he's trying to know as much as possible. And I would like him to know, too, that he should know from when he comes from. So little by little, when they get older, they will get in more and more involved to it. And I hope this will help too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would be the most important message that you want not only your grandchildren, but all the children of the world to know or keep in mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: To me, the most important thing is that people should not forget all those people that disappeared during the war. Because I am one of the survivors, and I'm very happy that I made it out. That's why I had a chance to make a family and have children of my own. But all my friends, I got nobody. And I don't want them to be forgotten. At least if people tell the story of what happened, people will remember those people that disappeared. And that's a very, very important message to me, I think. And that they should know what was going on because a lot of people don't even believe it that this-- that this thing happened. And at least I have firsthand account of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any other story that you want to tell?

speaker: interviewee
text: I probably will remember when the movie's over. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything about any of the special friends that you lost that you would like to be remembered?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, again, the friends that I lost is all kids from school. When I was-- you know, when I went away, I was 11 years old. So I had good friends like kids that lived next door to me. They're gone. They, they never came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, during the world in Belgium, we had to register. All the Jews had to go and register. So my name is Na-- my father-- my name is Nathan Wunderman, and I-- and I live there and there. And I'm married, and I have children. So everybody was registered as Jews, so they knew-- in case they wanted to get us together-- they knew where to find us, you see? Because in Belgium, you have to register where you live in the city. City hall, they have an address. They always can find you. Whenever you move, you have to a following address. You have to re-register. But during the war, they have to register all Jews. My father did not go. He says, if they have to find me, let them find me. But I'm not going to go in to tell them where I am. So the people say, hey, they're going to go, and they're going to take you. He said, so what? At least they don't know I'm Jewish. I have a chance. And don't go, either. He was telling the other people. But they went. They registered. They wouldn't listen. And my father, on account of this, had a good chance during the war because we had identity papers. And it was marked on the paper, born Poland. The other people was marked, born Poland, Jew-- a big stamp on their card. You follow me? So they didn't have no paper. They had to go false identities. But my father went on the same papers the whole war-- also Polish guy, but not a Jew. And that's the way he survived. For this, he was smart.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he went through the first war, and he was not a youngster, my father. So he had more experience what to do. He lived-- in his young years, he went to-- he lived in Israel. He went through pogroms in Israel there, and he was hunted down. He was sent back out of the country, went back to Poland. He went into the army in Poland. There he met my mother. And then from there, they went on the way to America. But they went as far as Belgium, and didn't have money to go further.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to stop to change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
